pattern: Put every distinct value in a set, then for each value check whether it can be the smallest element of a streak by repeatedly squaring it and testing membership — the streak starting at the smallest untried base gives the true streak length for that whole chain.
hints: Only the distinct values matter (duplicates never extend a streak), so drop them into a hash set for O(1) membership checks.
For a candidate starting value v, the rest of its streak is forced: it must contain v*v, then (v*v)*(v*v), and so on — walk that chain while each next square is present in the set, counting steps.
To avoid recomputing the same chain from its middle, only start counting from a value v whose square root is NOT itself present in the set — that guarantees v is the true start of its chain.

edge_inputs.description: no square relationship among any pair — no streak exists
one clean streak buried among unrelated numbers
minimal streak of exactly two elements
duplicate values must not inflate the streak length
two independent streaks of different lengths — the longer one wins
longest possible chain of repeated squaring starting from the smallest base value 2 (2, 4, 16, 256, 65536 all within the value range)
a value appears both as the square of one number and as a valid starting base for another chain of its own — must not double count or merge unrelated chains
starting the walk from the middle of a chain instead of its true root would undercount — the true start (3) must be found even though its square (9) appears earlier in the array

stress.description: large random array occasionally seeded with genuine square chains to exercise both the no-streak and long-streak paths at scale
